annotations.text: Superior mesenteric artery syndrome may occur in patients with anorexia nervosa, when loss of the fat that normally surrounds the neurovascular pedicle results in compression of the third portion of the duodenum between the aorta and the vertebral column.

The diagnostic considerations on admission included an abdominal emergency that was unrelated to anorexia nervosa, one that was related to anorexia, and chronic gastrointestinal complications of anorexia nervosa with rapidly increasing abdominal pain.

Factors associated with poor outcome include preadolescent age at onset, very low weight at presentation, poor state of functioning before the development of signs and symptoms of the disease, and substance abuse.
The  *Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders* , fourth edition, lists four diagnostic criteria for anorexia nervosa: two medical and two psychiatric
Superior mesenteric artery syndrome and perforation of the bowel needed to be ruled out immediately.

This patient consistently maintained an exceptionally low body weight, engaged in excessive physical activity despite his state of nutrition, and manifested extreme denial of the severity of the illness — all typical features of anorexia nervosa
Severe weight loss can also cause shrinkage of cardiac muscle mass, decreased left ventricular forces, diminished ejection fractions, and mitral-valve prolapse.
The patient's short stature and endocrine abnormalities were thus a consequence of anorexia nervosa.
Patients with an onset in their early teens and early exposure to treatment, such as this patient, have the best outcome, with a recovery rate of nearly 70% in some studies.
Low bone density is characteristic of anorexia nervosa, and studies show z scores below −1 at the spine and hip in 35 to 60% of adolescent boys with anorexia nervosa.
I was involved in this patient's care before and after the admission to this hospital.
The initial caloric intake is 300 kcal more than the patient's average 24-hour caloric intake just before admission.

However, future successes at school or jobs may improve his self-esteem, he may tire of the constraints of his illness, or he may begin to fear death; such factors could lead him to accept appropriate treatment.
